2
WELCOME FROM 
THE ACE COMMITTEE
Distinguished leaders from the Americas and beyond, 
We are honored to welcome you to the Americas Competitiveness Exchange 
(ACE) Panama 2024. ACE is a powerful mechanism that fosters cooperation 
and creation of new partnerships to strengthen economic development in 
the Americas. 
The 
ACE 
Committee 
comprises 
representatives 
from 
the 
General 
Secretariat of the Organization of American States (OAS) and the United 
States Government’s Department of Commerce (Economic Development 
Administration and International Trade Administration) and Department of 
State (Permanent Mission to the OAS). 
Throughout the week of January 29 to February 2, 2024, you will have the 
opportunity to explore and expand numerous collaborations with Panama’s 
vibrant industries, including transportation and logistics, transformative 
technologies, sustainable agriculture, clean energy, regenerative tourism, 
and more. 
We want to emphasize the importance of nurturing the connections you 
establish throughout the week to ensure successful outcomes. We are 
excited for what lies ahead of us and the collaboration and partnerships 
that will emerge from this week. To facilitate meaningful and strategic 
engagement throughout the program, we have compiled this trip book 
with key information about the ACE Panama agenda and participants. 
We encourage you to use this resource as a compass to identify 
potential collaborations and business partners for trade deals, research 
and development, new ventures, soft-landing arrangements, technical 
exchanges, and any other forms of collaboration. 
We wish to express our most sincere gratitude to Panama, our host, for the 
exceptional agenda they have prepared in collaboration with other national 
and international stakeholders. 
We look forward to our work together. 
Sincerely, 
The ACE Committee

ACE IMPACT
The ACE program is dedicated to achieving outcomes
through a data-driven approach. Throughout the planning phase, 
it diligently collects participant information and disseminates it for the 
purpose of identifying priority areas and prospects for collaboration. 
Leveraging data from diverse sources, it orchestrates a 
productive program. At the culmination of an event, 
ACE’s results conspicuously unveil significant 
linkages within strategic domains, 
thereby engendering collaborative 
prospects.
Since 2014

PANAMA 
OVERVIEW
 Strategic Location: Panama’s geographic location at the crossroads of North 
and South America and the Caribbean makes it a crucial transportation and 
logistics hub. The Panama Canal provides a vital link between the Atlantic and 
Pacific Oceans, facilitating global trade and attracting international shipping 
companies.
 Dollarized Economy: Panama uses the U.S. dollar as its official currency, 
providing stability and reducing currency exchange risks. This promotes foreign 
investment and makes it easier for international businesses to operate within 
the country.
 Growing Services Sector: Panama’s services sector, including finance, 
tourism, and logistics, has experienced significant growth in recent years. 
The country’s well-developed banking and financial services industry attracts 
international banks and makes Panama a regional financial center.
 Free Trade Agreements: Panama has an extensive network of free trade 
agreements (FTAs) with various countries including Canada, Chile, Peru, the 
United States, members of the European Union, and others. These agreements 
promote exports, facilitate market access for Panamanian products, and attract 
foreign direct investment (FDI).
 Colón Free Trade Zone: Panama’s Free Trade Zone is the second-
largest import-export platform in the world and the main 
free zone in the Western Hemisphere. It is one of the 
most important logistics centers for multimodal 
redistribution in the world. 
What Sets Panama Apart
4

5
ACE Panama Strategic Areas of Focus
 Transportation and Logistics
Panama 
is 
considered 
a 
logistics 
and 
transportation hub due to its strategic location and the 
presence of the Panama Canal. Sitting at the crossroads of 
North and South America, the Panama Canal provides a vital 
link between the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, facilitating global 
trade, and attracting international shipping companies. By leveraging 
its geographic advantage, maritime infrastructure, free trade zones, air 
connectivity, infrastructure development, and regional integration efforts, 
Panama has positioned itself as a key player in the global logistics industry.
 Information and 
Communication Technology (ICT) 
Panama is home to various research centers and 
businesses focused on Innovation and ICT. The ICT sector 
receives some of the highest FDI in the country. As part of 
the Panama Hub Digital strategy, the country seeks to make the 
ICT sector competitive at an international level through coordinated 
programs and projects. Panama has also strengthened ICT and cybersecurity 
skills throughout the public administration.
Panama provides a favorable legal framework that protects data privacy 
making it a natural destination to house data for large companies and 
governments. Additionally, Curie, a Google-owned subsea cable, added 
a branch in Panama, which will enhance connectivity and bandwidth to 
Central America.
 Advanced and 
Transformative Technologies 
Panama’s entrepreneurial spirit and innovation 
ecosystem are driving the growth of technology 
startups and digital businesses. The Panamanian government 
has shown a commitment to promoting the digital sector 
through various initiatives. This includes creating programs to foster 
entrepreneurship, providing incentives for technology investments, 
and developing policies to support the growth of the digital economy. 
In addition, Panama’s strategic location and well-established logistics 
infrastructure position it as a potential regional hub for technology services. 
The country seeks to attract regional headquarters of tech companies, 
IT outsourcing services, and data centers, leveraging its connectivity and 
business-friendly environment.

6
 Sustainable Water 
and Agriculture
Panama’s vulnerability to the effects of climate 
change makes sustainable water and agriculture a high 
priority for the country. To address potential challenges, 
Panama is interested in adopting measures that would promote 
sustainable agriculture and water management. The country is 
committed to fostering a blue and green economy through the sustainable 
use of natural resources. Panama recently became the first country in 
Latin America in acceding to the Convention on the Protection and Use of 
Transboundary Watercourses and International Lakes (Water Convention). 
Furthermore, the new State Agri-Food Policy Act represents a big step 
towards making its agricultural production more sustainable. The Act 
adopts an inclusive, territorial, and sustainable approach, designed to 
create jobs and promote the rural economy.
 Regenerative Tourism
Panama’s natural beauty, cultural heritage, and rich 
biodiversity make it a popular destination for nature 
enthusiasts and eco-tourists. Panama is actively promoting 
sustainable and responsible tourism initiatives. Its vision for 
regenerative tourism focuses on restoring and regenerating natural 
and cultural resources while providing meaningful experiences for travelers. 
In collaboration with local communities and stakeholders, the government 
is encouraging a focus on conserving biodiversity and minimizing negative 
environmental and socio-cultural impacts. This includes promoting eco-lodges, 
nature-based tours, and community-based tourism initiatives that provide 
economic benefits to local communities while preserving natural resources.
 Clean Energy 
Panama has been actively working to diversify 
its energy mix, reducing reliance on fossil fuels, 
and increasing the share of renewable energy sources. 
Currently, the primary sources of electricity generation in 
Panama include hydroelectric power, thermal power (including 
natural gas and oil), wind energy, and solar energy. The country’s 
favorable climate conditions and geographical location contribute to the 
viability of renewable energy projects.
Hydroelectric power is the largest source of electricity in Panama. It accounts 
for a significant portion of the country’s energy generation capacity and 
plays a crucial role in meeting the growing demand for electricity. Panama’s 
hydroelectric power potential enables it to generate surplus electricity that can 
be exported to neighboring countries. The country is part of the Central American 
Electrical Interconnection System (SIEPAC), a transmission infrastructure that 
enables the exchange of electricity among Central American countries. This 
interconnection enhances energy security, promotes efficiency, and facilitates 
the integration of renewable energy resources across the region.

7
Maritime University of Panama
The Miraflores Locks control all incoming and outgoing traffic on the Pacific side 
of the canal. Close to Panama City, the government established this impressive 
center for observing the engineering marvel. The visitor center features three 
observation decks providing a front-row view of canal traffic, the Panama Canal 
Museum, and four exhibition rooms detailing the history of the Panama Canal, its 
current operations, and the expansion project.
The Maritime University of Panama was founded in 1958. It exclusively trained 
polyvalent sailors until 1971. In 1972, the UMIP focus expanded to include careers for 
Deck and Engine Officers to meet the demand for International Merchant Marine 
Officers. The UMIP specializes in training seafarers for maritime, coastal, and 
merchant fleet roles, emphasizing life skills, leadership, discipline, responsibility, 
and values.
Panama Canal – Miraflores Locks 
ACE SITE VISITS

Panama Digital Gateway
City of Knowleadge
Panama Pacifico
The Panama Digital Gateway facilitates the convergence of submarine cables. It 
promotes an extensive ecosystem and serves as a hub for over-the-top (OTT) services 
and content distribution. It enhances data protection, enables diversification, and 
reduces round trip travel time to Central and South America. The new building 
offers 5,500 square meters of space, up to 650 racks, 3.5 megawatts of scalable 
power, and is designed with anti-seismic criteria.
The City of Knowleadge is a campus and community fostering knowledge-based 
activities. It serves as a hub for research, education, and innovation, hosting universities, 
research institutes, and international organizations. The goal is to promote sustainable 
development, innovation, and education in Panama and Latin America.
Panama Pacifico, the largest mixed development in Latin America, connects people, 
companies, and nature in a sustainable environment. As a special economic zone, 
it serves as a hub for manufacturing, logistics services, business activities, and non-
traditional services, including an ecological residential area.
8

Ascanio Arosemena Complex
Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise 
Authority (AMPYME)
The Ascanio Arosemena Training Center played a significant role in the events of 
January 9, 1964. It was a place where Panamanian students gathered to demand the 
raising of the national flag.
AMPYME’s rooms and spaces in Panama play a crucial role beyond being training 
places. These spaces serve as fundamental platforms promoting entrepreneurship, 
offering equipped facilities for business training, fostering skills acquisition, and 
providing strategic meeting points for businesspersons.
Technological University of Panama 
The Technological University of Panama 
focuses 
on 
producing 
qualified, 
entrepreneurial, and innovative individuals with integral formation, critical thinking, 
and social responsibility in engineering, sciences, and technology.
9

City of Arts
Wind Farm
The City of Arts is a public space where various activity programs, including theater, 
music, dance, and visual arts schools, are developed with a focus on accessibility.
The Wind Farm is located in the province of Coclé. It is the first in Panama and the 
largest in Central America, with 270 megawatts of installed power. Covering the 
energy demand of 6-7% of the population, it contributes to a significant reduction in 
greenhouse gas emissions.
Finca La Polola (Pink Lion S.A.)
The Finca la Polola (Pink Lion S.A.) is an agro-tourism project focused on producing 
quality exotic and tropical fruits in an environmentally friendly manner. The project 
combines recreational aspect with agricultural activities, including the sale of products 
like Persian lemon, Carica papaya, and pitahaya.
10

Agua Clara Visitor Center
Agua Clara Visitor Center offers an unparalleled view of Gatun Lake, where cargo ships 
and vessels transit every day. This center provides a unique experience enriched with 
data and history of this world-unifying route.
Manzanillo International Terminal (MIT)
Manzanillo International Terminal (MIT) serves as a distribution center for cargo 
destined for cities within Panama and nearby countries. It is one of the largest container 
transshipment terminals in the region with multimodal operations.
BioMuseo
BioMuseo, located on the Amador Causeway in Panama City, is a museum focusing on 
the natural history of Panama, highlighting its recent geological formation and impact 
on the ecology of the Western Hemisphere.
